data.content_mdx:
Before a robot can do anything, it needs to know where it is and what's around it. That's harder than it sounds.

## The 6 sensors a robotics engineer can't avoid

**Ultrasonic (HC-SR04 — ₹60)** — sends a sound pulse, measures the echo. Tells you distance. Cheap and reliable.

**Infrared (IR) — ₹40** — detects light reflections. Line followers and proximity sensing.

**Camera — ₹350+** — the most powerful and most complex sensor. A whole world of computer vision lives here.

**IMU (MPU-6050 — ₹180)** — accelerometer + gyroscope. Tells the robot which way is up, how fast it's accelerating, how it's rotating.

**Encoder — ₹250** — counts wheel rotations. Without one, your robot doesn't know how far it has moved.

**LiDAR (RPLIDAR A1 — ₹6,500)** — laser-based 3D mapping. The reason self-driving cars work.

## Sensor fusion

No single sensor is enough. A camera fails in the dark. An ultrasonic sensor can't see colour. Real robots combine 3–10 sensors at once and merge the data — this is called **sensor fusion**.

This is why the same robot can navigate a sunny street, a dark warehouse, and a foggy morning.

data.question: A line-following robot needs to detect a black line on a white floor. What's the simplest, cheapest sensor for the job?

options.explanation: Cameras work, but they're overkill (and need computer vision).
Ultrasonic measures distance, not colour reflectance.
Black absorbs IR, white reflects it. Perfect for line following.
LiDAR is way too expensive and complex for this.

cards.back: Detects light reflections — used in line followers
Accelerometer + gyroscope — knows up, down, rotation
Laser scanner that builds a 3D map
Combining multiple sensors to overcome single-sensor blind spots
